title: Drop spindle: the park-and-draft method
subtitle: A controlled drafting technique for beginners that separates spinning and drafting into two distinct steps
excerpt: Park-and-draft breaks the drop-spindle process into two stages: spin the spindle to load twist, park it between your knees or against your hip, then draft the fibre while the twist is held. Removes the coordination requirement of spinning and drafting simultaneously.
sourceNotes: The park-and-draft method is a widely taught introductory drop-spindle technique in US and UK spinning guilds. Observation-led; described in various open-access guild handouts and workshop notes. Not drawn from a specific PD source.

glossaryTerms.definition: The act of drawing fibres apart to thin them before twist is inserted. Drafting controls the thickness (grist) of the resulting yarn.
Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, ready for spinning.
The rotation inserted into fibres during spinning that causes them to bind together and form yarn. Z-twist rotates clockwise; S-twist rotates anticlockwise.

content.text: Park-and-draft separates spinning into two distinct actions. First, spin the spindle to add
. Then park the spindle against your body and
while the twist is held. This lets you focus entirely on drafting without managing the spindle at the same time. It is the standard introductory method taught in guild drop-spindle courses.

items.name: Drop spindle, top-whorl, 60 to 80 g weight (heavier spindles maintain momentum better for beginners)
Merino or Corriedale roving, 21 to 28 micron, 50 g
Leader yarn, approximately 50 cm of commercial wool yarn

content.text: Tie the leader yarn around the base of the spindle shaft just above the whorl. Wind 20 cm up the shaft in a figure-8 around the whorl, then bring it over the hook at the top and let 15 cm hang free. This is the leader: the commercial yarn you will spin the roving onto first.
Step 2: Join the roving to the leader

Untwist the end of the leader yarn slightly so the fibres splay out. Hold the roving against the splayed leader end, overlapping by 3 to 4 cm. Spin the spindle clockwise (for Z-twist) with your dominant hand. The twist will travel up into the overlap and lock the roving fibres to the leader. Hold the join firmly until it has taken twist and feels secure.

Spin the spindle clockwise with your dominant hand and let it drop, hanging free on the yarn. Watch it spin for 3 to 4 seconds to load twist into the short section of yarn near the top of the spindle. Now park the spindle: either tuck the shaft under your arm against your body, rest it against your knee, or grip the shaft between your knees. Both hands are now free. With both hands on the roving, pinch 2 to 3 cm above the hook with your upper hand, and draw the roving gently downward with your lower hand. The twist will rush into the drafted section when you release the upper pinch. Draft 5 to 10 cm at a time. Re-spin, park, draft. Repeat.

Wind on before the spindle reaches the floor (about 60 to 80 cm of yarn accumulated). Unhook the yarn, wind it onto the shaft in a cone shape above the whorl, re-hook, and continue. A spinning spindle that strikes the floor will unravel the twist in the last section.

Once park-and-draft produces yarn that holds together without breaking or over-twisting in the same spot, you are ready to try continuous spinning. Spin with the right hand and draft upward with the left, letting the spindle turn while you draft. Park-and-draft is the bridge; continuous spinning is the destination.

items.problem: The yarn snaps between the hook and the roving
items.cause: Over-twist; too many spins before drafting
items.fix: Draft more frequently: spin, park, draft a smaller distance. If the yarn breaks, rejoin by overlapping the broken end with fresh roving and re-adding twist as with the leader join.

items.problem: The twist does not travel up into the drafted section when the upper pinch is released
items.cause: The upper pinch is placed too high, or the yarn below is already over-twisted
items.fix: Pinch just 1 to 2 cm above the hook. The twist travels only as far as the nearest pinch point; place it close to the yarn so the twist has a short path to fill.

items.problem: The spindle reverses direction and unwinds
items.cause: The spindle ran out of momentum and gravity reversed it
items.fix: Spin again before the spindle has fully stopped. Beginners often wait too long between spin and park; the window for drafting is shorter than it looks.
